text: In this video we present the formation of a double barreled wet colostomy in a patient undergoing pelvic accentuation for a locally advanced rectal carcinoma.
Preoperative markings are made on the patient's abdomen for the vertical rectus abdominis myocutaneous (VRAM) flap, outlining the intended area for surgical incision.
The surgeon opens the abdomen through a midline incision, providing access to the underlying structures for the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon performs lateral to medial mobilization of the left hemicolon, carefully identifying the ureters during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a high tie of the inferior mesenteric artery, securing it with ligatures to control blood flow during the procedure.
The surgeon transects the sigmoid colon, ensuring sufficient length is preserved for the subsequent formation of a double barreled wet colostomy.
The surgeon fully mobilizes both ureters and proceeds to mobilize the rectum within the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
Following the mobilization of the bladder, the surgeon ligates the ureters at their most distal points, ensuring they are securely tied off.
Using the ureters as anatomical guides, the surgeon performs a beyond TME resection of the pelvic organs, carefully excising tissue beyond the standard total mesorectal excision boundaries.
The surgeon ligates the internal iliac artery bilaterally, securing the vessels on both sides within the pelvic cavity.
The patient is placed in the orthotomy position
The surgeon performs an extra sphincteric peroneal dissection, focusing on the area surrounding the sphincter muscles in the perineal region.
The surgeon continues dissection through the pelvic floor, connecting with the abdominal dissection to further expose the surgical field.
The surgeon delivers the resected specimen through the perineal wound following the completion of the anterior dissection in front of the prostate.
Following the removal of the specimen, the pelvic side wall structures are clearly visible, providing a detailed view of the anatomical features within the pelvic cavity.
The urinary reservoir of the double-barreled wet colostomy should be approximately ten centimeters.
The surgeon cuts the ureters to the appropriate length, preparing them for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon places a polysorb stay suture, securing tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis of the ureter to the colonic conduit using threomaxon sutures.
The surgeon carefully inserts a size eight infant feeding tube into the ureter, ensuring precise placement within the anatomical structure.
The surgeon transfixes the infant feeding tube within the ureter using Velosorb Rapide suture material, securing its position.
A pediatric feeding tube is inserted into the colonic conduit, with the intention of later pulling it out through the stoma.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis of the right ureter to the colonic conduit, using the same technique as previously described.
The video shows a clear view of the colonic conduit with both ureters visibly anastomosed to it.
The surgeon creates a trefine and brings the double-barreled colon conduit out over a Jax catheter, positioning it for further surgical manipulation.
This enables us to place the stoma bridge more safely.
The colonic conduit is positioned within the pelvis, showing no tension on the colon or ureters.
The surgeon opens the colostomy, revealing infant feeding tubes being brought out through the surgical site.
The surgeon flushes the infant feeding tubes and places them into a sterile glove for convenient handling during the remainder of the operation.
The surgeon develops the VRAM flap, which involves mobilizing the rectus abdominis muscle and its overlying skin and fat for reconstruction purposes.
The surgeon prepares to place the VRAM flap into the perineal cavity, ensuring a bulky and well-perfused closure of the perineum.
The surgeon prepares to perform simultaneous closure of the abdominal and perineal regions, ensuring both areas are securely closed.
The surgeon places a proline mesh over the large abdominal defect to reinforce the area, ensuring stability and support for the closure.
The surgeon completes the closure of the abdominal wound, revealing a matured stoma with typical appearance and positioning.
